cataloging_subject.label: Treatise on Garsing
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English prose component conventionally titled Treatise on Garsing, beginning with the final line of Hunter 307, f. 166r and continuing through f. 166v. It gives instructions for garsing (scarification) and ventosing (cupping). In the Hunter witness this component concludes the larger text identified by Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez as a Middle English On Bloodletting derived from Guy de Chauliac. Voigts's repertory treats the Hunter passage within VK 0659.00 but gives its three cited parallel versions separate VK 1841.00 entries. The component, its larger host work, Guy's Latin source tradition, four physical witnesses named by the U-M XML, Paul Schaffner's transcript, the later semi-diplomatic edition, and the digital representations remain distinct.

catalog_summary.title: Treatise on Garsing
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text
catalog_summary.date: Translated after 1363 and attested by the early fifteenth century; the earliest surviving witness is unresolved between Hunter 307 and the broadly dated Sloane 965 bloodletting section
catalog_summary.region: England; the Hunter witness language is tentatively localized to the Midlands, probably Huntingdonshire

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan CME item ID

titles.supplied_by: University of Michigan CME heading, following the MED stencil

assertion.notes: This is a modern conventional title; the manuscript passage has no independent title rubric.

titles.value: For garsyng and ventosyng it bifalliþ ofte tymes

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator
notes: The 2023 study states that the scribe and translator remain unidentified.

agents.name: Guy de Chauliac
notes: Author of the Chirurgia magna and of the larger On Bloodletting source text identified in the 2023 study; not the Middle English scribe or translator.

agents.name: Paul F. Schaffner
notes: Transcribed Hunter 307, ff. 166r-v in May 2020 and added markup in December 2020.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text

scope.description: Composite discovery display preserving the anonymous translation and the qualified attribution of its larger source text.

assertion.notes: This catalog display is not a claim that Guy produced the Middle English wording or that the conventional garsing component had independent authorial status.

scope.description: The Middle English passage conventionally cataloged as Treatise on Garsing.

assertion.notes: The attribution to an anonymous translator is inherited from the larger ff. 165v-166v On Bloodletting text in which this component occurs.

scope.description: Authorship of the larger source text and corresponding source passage, not of the Middle English translation as such.

assertion.notes: The 2023 study identifies ff. 165v-166v as Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting, from the Chirurgia magna. The U-M XML itself does not name Guy and treats the final component's repertory boundary as ambiguous.

scope.description: The raw manuscript transcript represented by the U-M CME item and repository XML.

notes: Schaffner is a modern transcriber, not the medieval author.

date_statements.display: After 1363 and by the early fifteenth century

scope.description: The Middle English translation represented by the Hunter passage.

assertion.notes: The lower bound is Guy's 1363 Chirurgia magna; the upper bound is the early-fifteenth-century Hunter witness. This brackets the translation rather than assigning an exact year.

date_statements.display: After 1363 and by the early fifteenth century; earliest surviving witness unresolved

scope.description: The Hunter 307 passage is early fifteenth century, while the relevant Sloane 965 bloodletting section has the broader fourteenth-fifteenth-century date; the evidence does not establish which surviving version is earlier.

assertion.notes: The lower bound follows Guy's 1363 source work rather than assigning Sloane 965 to a precise year within its broad catalog range. Hunter 307 supplies a secure terminus by the early fifteenth century, but neither witness is designated the confirmed earliest attestation.

places.label: Huntingdonshire
notes: A probable localization of the Hunter witness language, not a demonstrated place of composition or translation.
No exact place of translation is established.

scope.description: The language of Hunter 307, ff. 165v-166v, including the cataloged final component.

assertion.notes: The 2023 eLALME analysis places the witness language broadly in the Midlands and probably in Huntingdonshire. It is not transferred to the anonymous translator's biography or the work's composition event.

form_statements.description: Continuous instructional medical prose; editorial headings and the U-M argument do not change the work's form.

assertion.notes: The passage gives procedural directions for scarification and cupping and derives from Guy de Chauliac's surgical tradition.
Translation status is established for the larger On Bloodletting text; the exact Latin exemplar and component boundary remain unknown.
The component belongs to the larger On Bloodletting text, although its immediate instructions concern scarification and ventosing.
The passage maps procedures to pains and ailments of the eyes, head, jaw, mouth, teeth, neck, shoulders, arms, chest, back, buttocks, and skin.

summaries.value: A short Middle English medical prose passage explaining that small, hidden veins may be treated by garsing (scarification) or ventosing (cupping), then specifying body sites for treating pains and ailments of the eyes, head, mouth, neck, shoulders, limbs, chest, back, and skin. It ends with the practitioner's defense of the work and claim to have cured many patients. In Hunter 307 it is the final component of a larger text on bloodletting. The encoded item also retains modern U-M title matter, a Schaffner byline and date, and an editorial argument about its source and parallel witnesses.

CME302 begins at Hunter 307, f. 166r, line 26, not at the opening of On Bloodletting on f. 165v, line 14. The modern heading Treatise on Garsing and the repertory's separate treatment of parallel versions justify discovery as a component, while the parent relationship remains explicit.

extent.description: One cataloged medieval prose component in one XML DIV1 and one body P, plus a separately modeled modern U-M editorial frame consisting of two heads, an opener/byline/dateline, and one argument paragraph. The work begins on the final line of Hunter 307, f. 166r and continues through f. 166v. The paratext has item_count 0 and is excluded from the intrinsic work count.

content_parts.label: U-M editorial framing
content_parts.summary: Modern encoding-level title matter, Schaffner byline and May 2020 dateline, and an argument identifying the source locus, repertory boundary, and three collateral witnesses.
notes: This retained modern paratext is represented for item-boundary accounting but is not part of the medieval work, does not make the work multilingual, and does not make Schaffner its author.

content_parts.label: Treatise on Garsing
content_parts.summary: The full cataloged prose component, from its instruction about hidden small veins through the closing practitioner testimonial.

locators.value: f. 166r, line 26-f. 166v; semi-diplomatic edition on printed pp. 117 and 119
Figures 14-15, printed pp. 116 and 118; Hunter 307, ff. 166r-v

notes: The work boundary is intellectual and repertorial; the manuscript does not supply a separate rubric at the opening.

related_works.title: On Bloodletting
notes: The larger Middle English text begins at Hunter 307, f. 165v, line 14 and ends at f. 166v. CME302 contains only its final garsing and ventosing component.

related_works.title: Chirurgia magna
notes: This relation is scoped conceptually to the source passage represented by the cataloged component. The anonymous translator's exact Latin exemplar has not been identified.

related_works.title: For garsing and ventosing it befalleth ofttimes small veins to be touched
notes: The U-M XML says Voigts gives the Royal 18 A VI, Sloane 965, and Sloane 3486 parallels separate VK 1841.00 entries while treating the Hunter passage inside VK 0659.00. This record preserves, rather than resolves, that boundary difference.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Treatise on garsing, CME302 XML, transcribed by Paul F. Schaffner
evidence.summary: Supplies the conventional titles, identifiers, direct Hunter 307 source statement, folio range, 2020 transcription and markup dates, public-domain assertion, Voigts/eVK2 identifiers and parallel witnesses, prose text, incipit, and U-M publication date.

evidence.citation: Laura Esteban-Segura and Carlos Soriano-Jiménez, 'Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v),' Revista Canaria de Estudios Ingleses 87 (2023): 107-130
evidence.summary: Identifies the larger ff. 165v-166v text as a Middle English On Bloodletting by Guy de Chauliac, dates and describes the Hunter witness, states that the scribe-translator is anonymous, supplies a semi-diplomatic edition and plates, maps the CME component's opening at f. 166r line 26, identifies Sloane 3486 as another version, and tentatively localizes the witness language to Huntingdonshire.

evidence.citation: University of Glasgow Archives and Special Collections, GB 247 MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1), item record 296678
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmark, holding institution, physical extent, vellum support, twenty-six-line single columns, missing ff. 147-148, access contact, provenance, legacy descriptions, Doyle's early-fifteenth-century hand correction, and the 2023 publication citation. Its two canvases are selected sample images, not a complete facsimile.

evidence.citation: The Málaga Corpus of Late Middle English Scientific Prose, WebHunter, Hunter 307 description and manuscript image/text sequences
evidence.summary: The official project exposes Hunter 307 as separate JPEG images and diplomatic transcriptions in three editorial viewer sequences of 329, 100, and 38 slots. The first 98 hu307p2 slots duplicate hu307p1 surfaces from f. 115r through f. 165v, and hu307p3 also repeats full-folio JPEGs across paired left/right transcription slots; the counts are not unique images or physical surfaces. Direct checks map CME302 to hu307p2 images pag99-c.jpg-pag100-c.jpg and transcription files page98.txt-page99.txt. WebHunter's description says f. 161 is missing even though its own viewer exposes f. 161r-v; the current Glasgow catalog instead reports missing ff. 147-148. The provider's Copyright page restricts copying and image reuse.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Sloane MS 965, record 040-002113317
evidence.locator: bloodletting tracts ff. 156v-164v; CME302 parallel ff. 163v-164v
evidence.summary: The current contents record dates the English bloodletting tracts on ff. 156v-164v to the fourteenth-fifteenth century. Because that range overlaps or may precede Hunter 307's early-fifteenth-century range, the surviving earliest witness cannot be assigned to Hunter alone.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, cūren v.(2), quotation from a1450 *Treat.Garsing (Htrn 307), f. 166v
evidence.locator: sense and quotation citing Treat.Garsing (Htrn 307), f. 166v
evidence.summary: Supports the established MED stencil title, approximate a1450 dating convention, Hunter shelfmark abbreviation, folio locator, and a quotation from the closing practitioner testimonial.

evidence.citation: John Young and P. Henderson Aitken, A Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of the Hunterian Museum in the University of Glasgow (Glasgow, 1908), pp. 245-246
evidence.summary: Provides the legacy detailed codex description and historical shelfmarks. Its fourteenth-century date and original-binding claim are retained as older catalog assertions rather than preferred over Doyle's later early-fifteenth-century hand and mid-sixteenth-century binding assessment.

open_questions.question: Should the Hunter garsing passage be cataloged as an independently circulating work/version, or only as the final component of On Bloodletting?
next_steps: Consult the complete current eVK2 records v0034490000, v0057530000, v0088010000, and v0096920000.
Compare the exact openings, closings, and surrounding text in Royal 18 A VI, Sloane 965, and Sloane 3486.
Retain the current component-plus-parent model until repertory or textual evidence resolves whether VK 1841 is an independent work or a portable section.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin passage and physical exemplar underlie the Middle English translation represented in Hunter 307?
next_steps: Collate the Hunter text against critical editions and manuscripts of the phlebotomy material in Guy de Chauliac's Chirurgia magna.
Distinguish textual-source identification from identification of the translator's physical exemplar.

notes: The 2023 article calls its edition the first edition of the witness, although Schaffner made the U-M transcript in 2020 and the U-M item header dates public distribution to 2023. The record therefore avoids asserting an unqualified first-publication priority between the two 2023 appearances.
The article's Huntingdonshire finding describes the language of ff. 165v-166v; it is not treated as a secure place of translation or composition.
The Glasgow catalog's broad legacy date and binding description are preserved as attributed historical claims, not silently reconciled with Doyle's later assessment.
